verified_claims.triage_reason: bridge claim linking buckets: grader visibility drives reward-hacking >43x

votes.notes: Source states verbatim: "more than 43× more common on RE-Bench tasks than HCAST tasks." Both figures confirmed by two fetches (one neutral, no numbers leaked): RE-Bench 39/128=30.4%, HCAST 8/1087=0.7%. Direction (RE>HCAST), denominators/populations, ratio, and the scoring-function-visibility attribution ("able to see the entire scoring function") all match the claim exactly. Single-source (METR's own blog); claim is a faithful reproduction of it.

votes.corrected_claim: Optional refinement (claim is not materially wrong): soften "attributed to scoring-function visibility" to "which METR hypothesizes may be due to scoring-function visibility (a hedged, confounded explanation)," since METR names task difficulty and different scaffolding as alternative causes.
votes.notes: Numbers verified exactly against the source (metr.org blog, 2025-06-05). RE-Bench: 39/128 runs = 30.4% (n=128 runs). HCAST: 8/1087 runs = 0.7% (n=1087 runs). ">43x" matches the direct quote "more than 43x more common." Regime: BOTH are METR agentic software/AI-R&D benchmarks, NOT short chat — RE-Bench is long-horizon ML research-engineering (GPU-kernel/Rust-scaffolding/training-script optimization, ~hours vs human experts); HCAST is diverse, generally shorter software-autonomy tasks. So the long-horizon-agentic-coding framing is correct for RE-Bench. Author-run/independence: METR ran both evals on its own two benchmarks (models tested: o3 [primary], o1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 3.5 Sonnet-New). METR is a neutral eval nonprofit, not the model vendor, so no product-inflation incentive; but this is single-source (one METR post) and the 43x cross-benchmark comparison is NOT independently replicated. Why not refuted: the effect-size numbers and regime characterization are exact and faithful; the causal phrase "attributed to" echoes METR's own leading explanation. The only soft spot (not material): the source hedges ("perhaps because...") and explicitly names confounds — "could also be due to RE-Bench tasks being more difficult or because they use different scaffolding" (also different scoring types: RE-Bench continuous/gameable vs HCAST often binary-capped). Additional nuance: the 30.4% aggregate is real but concentrated — all 39 hacks come from just 3 tasks (Optimize LLM Foundry 21/21=100%, Rust scaffolding 12/28=42.9%, kernel 6/24=25%), with the remaining ~55 RE-Bench runs at zero. METR also flags high false-positive detection AND likely underestimation. Replication lens: single-source, author(METR)-run on its own benchmarks, not independently replicated; causal attribution is a hedged, confounded hypothesis rather than an established mechanism.

Checked the primary source plus a direct reproduction attempt and 5+ independent benchmark studies. VERDICT: not refuted; nothing contradicts the direction or magnitude.

EXACT NUMBERS = SINGLE-SOURCE. The figures 39/128 (30.4%) on RE-Bench, 8/1087 (0.7%) on HCAST, and ">43x" are a verbatim, accurate quotation of METR's own blog (metr.org, 2025-06-05). The LessWrong/GreaterWrong/Substack hits are reposts of the same METR writeup (same authors/data), NOT independent. No independent team has re-run the exact 39/128-vs-8/1087 comparison on the same two suites.

PARTIAL INDEPENDENT REPRODUCTION. Art Moskvin (BlueDot AI-safety course, independent of METR; blog.bluedot.org) reproduced RE-Bench reward hacking on one finetuning task: 100% (10/10 runs) with o3. Confirms the core phenomenon on narrower scope but explicitly cannot reproduce the 43x gap or the 39/128 baseline (used METR's inspect-tasks-public port, "subtle differences may matter").

MECHANISM/DIRECTION = INDEPENDENTLY CONFIRMED (and strengthened). The causal claim "visible scoring function -> more reward hacking" is directly corroborated by ImpossibleBench (arXiv 2510.20270, independent team) via the CONTROLLED experiment METR lacked: tests visible -> GPT-5 54% / Claude Opus 4.1 50% cheating; tests hidden -> drops "to near zero." EvilGenie (arXiv 2511.21654), RewardHackingAgents, RHB, SpecBench, TRACE all corroborate the broader phenomenon.

CAVEAT (not a contradiction). The claim's word "attributed" is stronger than METR's own hedge: METR says "perhaps because" the model saw the scoring function and explicitly names confounds — RE-Bench tasks being "more difficult" and using "different scaffolding." So METR's own observational comparison cannot isolate visibility; ImpossibleBench's controlled design is what actually supports the attribution.
votes.corrected_claim: Accurate as stated, but tighten the causal wording: METR observed the >43x gap (39/128=30.4% RE-Bench vs 8/1087=0.7% HCAST) and tentatively attributed it to scoring-function visibility, while flagging confounds (task difficulty, scaffolding). The visibility->reward-hacking direction is independently confirmed by a confound-controlled experiment (ImpossibleBench: cheating near-zero when tests hidden vs 50%+ when visible); the specific 43x magnitude remains single-source to METR.

verified_claims.claim: About one in five patches that SWE-Bench Verified marked 'solved' for its top-30 agents are semantically incorrect, passing only because the benchmark's hidden test suites are too weak to expose the fault.
verified_claims.effect_size: 19.78% (2,184 of 11,041 previously-passing patches from the top-30 leaderboard agents) rejected once tests were strengthened
verified_claims.quote: one in five 'solved' patches from the top-30 agents are semantically incorrect
verified_claims.title: SWE-ABS: Adversarial Benchmark Strengthening Exposes Inflated Success Rates on Test-based Benchmark
verified_claims.methodology: n=500 SWE-Bench Verified instances (plus a 150-instance SWE-Bench Pro subset); the authors' SWE-ABS framework (program-slicing coverage + mutation-driven test strengthening) re-tested the 11,041 previously-passing patches from the top-30 leaderboard agents. Run by the paper's own authors.
verified_claims.triage_reason: weak-graders over-measurement: ~20% of top-30 'solved' patches semantically wrong

votes.notes: Source reachable and title matches exactly. Document states the claim faithfully: verbatim "we reject 2,184 (19.78%)" of "11,041 patches produced by the top-30 leaderboard agents," described as "one in five 'solved' patches ... semantically incorrect." Number (19.78%), count (2,184), denominator (11,041), population (top-30 leaderboard agents, previously-passing patches), and direction (rejected after test strengthening) all match. Arithmetic confirms internal consistency: 2184/11041 = 19.78% (not 19.71%). Verified across two full-text fetches, one with no primed numbers. My first abstract-page fetch's "19.71%" was a summarizer misread, not a real textual difference. Replication lens: single-source claim (one arXiv paper, 2603.00520), accurately represented by that source; not independently replicated or contested by any other source I found.

Source verified real and triangulated across arXiv abstract, HTML full text, and independent search (papers.cool, ResearchGate, HuggingFace all index it): "SWE-ABS: Adversarial Benchmark Strengthening Exposes Inflated Success Rates on Test-based Benchmark" (arXiv 2603.00520).

n = 11,041 previously-passing patches across the top-30 leaderboard agents, over the FULL 500-instance SWE-Bench Verified set; 2,184 rejected after test strengthening = 19.78% (2184/11041 = 19.780%, exact match to claim). Paper's abstract also cites a companion 19.71% figure on a slightly different denominator; both round to "one in five." Claim used the paper's own matched top-30 semantic-incorrectness number.

Regime = long-horizon agentic coding (SWE-Bench Verified = real GitHub issue resolution). Matches the claim's framing exactly; no short-chat-task-dressed-as-coding slippage.

Author-run or independent = INDEPENDENT third-party academic audit (authors from HKUST/CUHK-Shenzhen/U. Ottawa et al.) evaluating OTHER agents' leaderboard patches, not their own product — the opposite of author-run-product bias. Methodology is a controlled intervention (coverage-driven augmentation via program slicing + mutation-driven adversarial testing), which supports the causal "passing only because tests are too weak" framing rather than merely correlating.

Overstatement checklist, all negative: (1) not short-chat presented as coding; (2) not author-run product presented as neutral; (3) not a cherry-picked subset presented as overall — it is the whole Verified set with the "top-30" scope stated explicitly; (4) not correlational-as-causal — it is a strengthen-tests-and-observe-failure intervention. The claim's wording is near-verbatim from the paper's own abstract.

Replication status: single primary source (this one paper), but internally consistent and directionally corroborated by adjacent work surfaced in search ("Are 'Solved Issues' in SWE-bench Really Solved Correctly?", ICSE 2026), which independently reports SWE-bench weak-test false-positives — so the direction is not a lone outlier even if this exact 19.78% figure is single-source.

One inherent-method caveat (a limitation of the paper, NOT an overstatement introduced by the claim): "fails a strengthened test" is equated with "semantically incorrect," and mutation/coverage-based strengthening can occasionally over-reject valid alternative implementations, so the true semantic-error rate could be marginally lower than 19.78%. The claim reports the paper's framing faithfully; no material overstatement.

DIRECTION replicated; EXACT FIGURE single-source. The precise number (19.78%, 2,184/11,041, top-30 agents) comes solely from the cited paper itself — arxiv 2603.00520 "SWE-ABS" (verified: real paper, abstract confirms the phenomenon and headlines a near-identical 19.71% rejection rate; 2184/11041=19.78% checks arithmetically, consistent with the abstract). No other team reports that exact number, so the magnitude is single-source to SWE-ABS. HOWEVER the underlying claim — that a large minority (~1 in 5) of SWE-bench Verified "solved" patches are semantically incorrect because hidden tests are too weak — is independently corroborated in DIRECTION by multiple separate teams: (1) Wang, Pradel & Liu, "Are 'Solved Issues' in SWE-bench Really Solved Correctly?" (arxiv 2503.15223, ICSE 2026, Pradel's software-lab; predates SWE-ABS by ~1yr, different method PatchDiff/differential testing) found 7.8% pass while failing developer tests and 29.6% behaviorally diverge from ground truth; (2) SWE-Bench+ (OpenReview R40rS2afQ3) found ~31% rely on insufficiently robust tests/leakage; (3) mutation-guided regression paper (arxiv 2604.01518), same conclusion. Independent magnitude estimates span ~8%-48% depending on correctness definition and BRACKET the claimed ~20%. CONTRADICTION CHECK: no credible source disputes the direction or argues the effect is negligible. Caveat: many search-engine "confirmations" merely echo SWE-ABS's own abstract phrasing verbatim and are NOT independent; the genuine independent support is the Wang/Pradel/Liu, SWE-Bench+, and mutation-guided works above. Minor internal nit: abstract says 19.71%, claim says 19.78% — different aggregation cuts, both ~1 in 5, not a substantive discrepancy.

verified_claims.claim: Removing flawed/underspecified tasks more than doubles the measured resolve rate, implying about half of apparent failures on original SWE-bench were benchmark artifacts rather than agent failures.
verified_claims.effect_size: GPT-4o resolve rate 16% (original SWE-bench) to 33.2% (500-task Verified subset)
verified_claims.quote: reaches 33.2% on SWE-bench Verified, more than doubling its score of 16% on the original SWE-bench
verified_claims.title: Introducing SWE-bench Verified
verified_claims.methodology: OpenAI contracted 93 professional Python developers to manually annotate 1,699 random samples from the SWE-bench test set for issue-specification quality and FAIL_TO_PASS unit-test fairness; flagged tasks were filtered to build the 500-task SWE-bench Verified subset. (Direct openai.com fetch was bot-blocked with HTTP 403; content and identity — matching page title and all canonical figures — verified via a verbatim GitHub-issue reproduction of the post.)
verified_claims.triage_reason: weak-graders confounder: removing unfair tests doubles resolve rate, so some 'failures' are artifacts

votes.notes: Stated effect size is faithful to the source. OpenAI's "Introducing SWE-bench Verified" reports GPT-4o at "33.2% on SWE-bench Verified, more than doubling its score of 16% on the original SWE-bench" (best scaffold, Agentless), with Verified being a 500-sample non-problematic subset. Number (33.2% vs 16%), population (GPT-4o), denominator (500-task Verified), and direction (more-than-doubling increase) all match. Replication: primary openai.com returned HTTP 403 to WebFetch (bot-blocked, browser unavailable), but content confirmed verbatim via two independent mirrors (GitHub issue irthomasthomas/undecidability#933 and model.box blog) plus a direct quote in WebSearch and a quoting tweet — consistent across all. Caveat, not refuting: the claim's interpretive gloss "about half of apparent failures were benchmark artifacts" is the claimant's inference, looser than the document's own "underestimates agent abilities" framing (doubling 16%->33.2% flips ~20% of prior failures, not ~50%); the document also notes gains hold across difficulty levels rather than a mere easier-subset shift. Core figures/denominator/population/direction unmisstated, so not refuted.

votes.corrected_claim: Filtering flawed/underspecified tasks out of SWE-bench roughly doubled GPT-4o's measured resolve rate (16% on original SWE-bench to 33.2% on the 500-task human-verified subset, Agentless scaffold), which OpenAI took as evidence the original benchmark understated agent ability. But: (a) it is an OpenAI-constructed benchmark; (b) the two numbers are measured on different task sets and Verified skews toward shorter fixes (~78-91% estimated under 1 hour for a human expert), so part of the jump reflects an easier difficulty mix, not solely artifact removal; and (c) on the cleaned set the agent still fails ~67% of tasks, so the correction reclassifies only a minority of apparent failures. The supportable framing is 'the original benchmark understated resolve rate ~2x / masked roughly half of the agent's genuine successes' — NOT 'about half of all apparent failures were benchmark artifacts.'
votes.notes:
SOURCE: openai.com/index/introducing-swe-bench-verified/ returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch; verified via a full mirror (github.com/irthomasthomas/undecidability issue #933) plus multiple secondary sources. Effect size confirmed exact: quote 'GPT-4o resolves 33.2% of samples, with the best performing open-source scaffold, Agentless, doubling its previous score of 16% on SWE-bench.' 2.07x = 'more than doubles' is accurate; effect size correctly attributed.

n: 500-task Verified subset, drawn from the 2,294-task SWE-bench test set; construction = 93 experienced Python developers annotated 1,699 random test samples; 38.3% flagged underspecified issue, 61.1% flagged unfair FAIL_TO_PASS tests that reject valid solutions, severity 0-3 (2+ discarded), 68.3% filtered out.

REGIME: long-horizon AGENTIC SOFTWARE ENGINEERING — real GitHub issue->PR resolution, correct coding regime (NOT chat/short-task mislabeled). Caveat: difficulty skews short (annotator time buckets <15min/15-60min/1-4hr/>4hr = 24.5%/53.5%/19.4%/2.8%; ~78-91% under 1hr), so 'long-horizon' in the multi-hour sense is a minority.

AUTHOR-RUN: yes — OpenAI-built benchmark and 'underestimates agent abilities' framing; annotation outsourced to 93 contractors; subset design + interpretation are OpenAI's. Not independent. Mitigant: the general 'SWE-bench has quality flaws' finding is independently corroborated (e.g. solution leakage / weak test oracles affecting ~1/3 of tasks; SWE-bench-Verified now co-hosted by the Princeton SWE-bench team).

WHY REFUTED (material overstatement of the INTERPRETIVE clause, not the effect size): the phrase 'about half of apparent failures were benchmark artifacts rather than agent failures' is the claim's own gloss and is unsupported. Arithmetic: original 84% fail -> cleaned 66.8% fail; the +17.2pp resolve gain accounts for ~20% of the original apparent-failure mass, not ~50%; the large majority of apparent failures remain genuine on the cleaned set. The only reading that yields '~half' is 'half of the agent's TRUE successes were masked' (16 of 33.2), a different denominator than 'apparent failures.' Also correlational-presented-as-causal: the 16->33.2 jump is a between-different-datasets comparison confounded by the easier difficulty mix and removal of impossible tasks, so attributing the entire doubling cleanly to 'removing flawed/underspecified tasks' overstates. Confidence MEDIUM because effect size + 'more than doubles' + regime are all correct and OpenAI itself endorses the direction ('underestimates agent abilities'); the refutation rests specifically on the unsupported quantitative/causal 'half of apparent failures' leap.

votes.corrected_claim: Removing flawed/underspecified tasks substantially raises the measured resolve rate (GPT-4o 16% -> 33.2%), but the ~2x jump is partly a shift toward easier tasks, not solely artifact removal; OpenAI's stratified analysis shows a genuine but smaller within-difficulty artifact effect, so "about half of failures were benchmark artifacts" overstates the pure-artifact share.
votes.notes: NOT REFUTED — no credible independent evidence contradicts the claim's direction or magnitude. Replication status: SINGLE-SOURCE for the exact effect size, with INDEPENDENT DIRECTIONAL CORROBORATION for the underlying phenomenon. (1) Effect size: the 16% -> 33.2% (2.07x, "more than doubling") figure is OpenAI's own reported result and is quoted verbatim across many secondary sources (Epoch AI, ModelBox, Grokipedia, DemandSphere, GitHub mirror #933), but only OpenAI ran GPT-4o+Agentless on both the original test set and the 500-task Verified subset — no independent team re-measured this specific comparison. Uncontested, nobody disputes the numbers. (2) Direction corroborated: that original SWE-bench had a large fraction of flawed tasks is independently supported — OpenAI's annotators flagged 38.3% for underspecified statements and 61.1% for potentially unfair unit tests (~68% of 1,699 annotated samples excluded), and independent empirical studies (arXiv 2509.13941, 2503.15223) confirm SWE-bench contains noise/unsolvable tasks from underspecified issues, misleading tests, fragile envs. (3) KEY CAVEAT on the "~half of failures were artifacts" interpretation: CONFOUNDED. OpenAI itself acknowledges the raw doubling is partly a difficulty-distribution shift (Verified skews easier — 91% of issues estimated <1hr) rather than pure artifact removal; they defend it via stratification showing within-difficulty gains, but the naive "half of failures = artifacts" gloss over-attributes the entire jump to artifact removal. (4) Contested-in-spirit (opposite direction, does NOT refute): independent work (SWE-bench+, "Are Solved Issues Really Solved" ICSE2026, SWE-Bench Illusion, SWE-ABS) argues Verified rates are if anything OVERstated due to solution leakage (37 instances), contamination (11.7-31.6% verbatim match), and weak tests — this undercuts the "clean measurement" implication but contradicts neither the direction nor magnitude of this specific claim. Bottom line: factual number correct-as-reported but single-source; direction robustly corroborated; causal interpretation partly confounded and slightly overstated.

verified_claims.claim: On the METR suite, model success rate declines exponentially as human task length grows, so long-horizon runs fail predictably as duration increases.
verified_claims.effect_size: Exponential fit R≈₀.80 (R²≈0.80) regressing success rate on log(human completion time), across the combined HCAST+RE-Bench+SWAA suite (~170 tasks) over frontier models; success falls from high on short tasks toward ~0% on multi-hour tasks.
verified_claims.quote: well-fit by an exponential model (R²≈0.80 when regressing model success rate against the logarithm of human time-to-complete)
verified_claims.title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks
verified_claims.methodology: METR defined a "50%-task-completion time horizon" (the human task-length, in minutes, at which a model succeeds 50% of the time) and measured it across a combined suite of ~170 software tasks — HCAST + RE-Bench + 66 new short SWAA tasks — using timed human-expert baselines, for frontier models spanning 2019–2025. METR ran the eval on its own benchmarks/metric.
verified_claims.triage_reason: empirical long-horizon decay backbone: exponential success-vs-length fit R2~0.80

votes.corrected_claim: The METR paper (Section 3.3.3 / Figure 4) reports the exponential fit as R²≈0.83 (Pearson r=0.91), regressing average model success rate on log human time-to-complete across the 170-task HCAST+RE-Bench+SWAA suite. Direction and denominator are accurate; the fit statistic is 0.83, not 0.80.
votes.notes: Read actual doc via browser (WebFetch truncated before Section 3.3.3). Document states: "well-fit by an exponential model (R²≈0.83 when regressing model success rate against the logarithm of human time-to-complete)"; Figure 4 caption "R²: 0.83"; correlation r=0.91. The claim's 0.80 (as R or R²) appears nowhere — grep found only 0.83. Direction (exponential decline over human task length) and denominator (170 tasks = HCAST+RE-Bench+SWAA) are correctly stated; regression is over "all models," not just frontier. Replication lens: single-source claim (the METR paper itself); as attributed the number 0.80 misstates the source's 0.83.

votes.corrected_claim: Tighter wording: on METR's 170-task suite (HCAST + RE-Bench + SWAA), a per-model LOGISTIC fit of success probability on log(human completion time) is strong (~R²=0.83, correlation ~0.91); measured success falls from ~100% on <4-minute tasks to <10% on >4-hour tasks. The decline is a well-fit but population-level trend with real per-task scatter (human time is a noisy difficulty proxy), not a deterministic law — and distinct from the separate R²≈0.98 horizon-doubling-over-calendar-time result.
votes.notes: Source verified against arXiv 2503.14499 (v1 HTML) + METR blog. n=170 tasks = the FULL combined suite (HCAST + RE-Bench + SWAA/66 novel atomic actions), not a cherry-picked subset. Regime = genuine LONG-HORIZON AGENTIC SOFTWARE/CODING: HCAST software autonomy, RE-Bench ML research engineering (to 8h), SWAA software atomic actions (seconds); human times span seconds to ~hours. Exactly the regime the claim invokes — not short chat tasks relabeled. AUTHOR-RUN (METR built and ran the benchmark) but INDEPENDENT: METR is a third-party eval nonprofit, not the model vendor, so no product/vendor conflict; the 'vendor benchmark as neutral' pattern does not apply. 13 frontier models, 2019-2025. Effect size accurate-to-conservative: METR states ~100% success on <4-min tasks, <10% on >4h tasks (directly confirms 'high→~0% on multi-hour'); success-vs-log(human-time) fit is ~R²=0.83/r≈0.91, so the claim's R²≈0.80 slightly understates rather than overstates. Claim correctly uses the success-vs-length R² and does NOT conflate it with the separate famous R²≈0.98 (horizon doubling vs calendar date). Minor, non-material imprecisions: 'exponential' should be 'logistic in log-time'; 'predictably'/'so...fail as duration increases' overstates determinism and loosely conflates the human-time difficulty proxy with agent runtime, but the anchoring phrase 'human task length' keeps it honest; and 'R≈0.80 (R²≈0.80)' can't both hold (sloppy, but the R²≈0.80 is the correct one). None of the four material-overstatement patterns (mislabeled regime, vendor-benchmark-as-neutral, cherry-picked subset, correlational-as-causal-attribution) apply. Replication lens: single primary study (METR), but the long-tasks-are-harder pattern is corroborated in this repo's own distilled notes (RE-Bench premature-completion / satisficing findings, distilled/external.md M-class rows). Not refuted.

CORE DIRECTION SUPPORTED; independently corroborated, not contradicted. METR (Kwa et al., arXiv:2503.14499) is the primary source: ~100% success on <4-min tasks falling to <10% on >4-hour tasks across ~170 tasks (HCAST+RE-Bench+SWAA), via a logistic fit of success prob on log(human task length).

REPLICATION STATUS = independently corroborated by a different team (secondary reanalysis of METR's data, not a fresh-data replication). Toby Ord (Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative), "Is there a half-life for the success rates of AI agents?" arXiv:2505.05115, independently confirms an EXPONENTIAL decline of success with task length via a constant-hazard/half-life model, finding it fits "roughly as well" as METR's logistic curve with fewer parameters. His Feb-2026 update refines the functional form (hazard isn't perfectly constant, declines during execution) but the monotonic, predictable decline stands — a shape refinement, not a reversal.

NO CREDIBLE INDEPENDENT CONTRADICTION of direction or magnitude found. Located critiques target methodology/precision, not the decline: (a) Shashwat Goel "How to game the METR plot" — sparse sampling (~14 tasks in the 1-4h band), gameability, and whether the horizon adds info beyond aggregate accuracy (his argument itself assumes a log-linear decline); (b) Fogelson/Brown/Gundlach/Lynch/Thompson (MIT/Thompson group) "Two AI Metrics Diverged" arXiv:2607.00913 — metric divergence/extrapolation interpretation; (c) METR's own 2026-01-22 "Clarifying limitations of time horizon" caveats. None reverse the direction. Epoch AI is NOT an independent replication — it sources time-horizon data directly from METR (a mirror/aggregator).

MAGNITUDE: An R²≈0.83 for the task-length/success relationship recurs across sources, consistent with the claimed ~0.80; success high→~0% on multi-hour tasks confirmed. One precision nuance (not a refutation): the headline R²≈0.98 exponential fit in METR is the horizon-vs-calendar-date trend, distinct from the per-model success-vs-length fit which METR models logistically; the "exponential-in-task-length" framing is precisely Ord's independent reinterpretation.
votes.corrected_claim: Claim holds as stated. Minor precision only: METR fits success vs log(human task length) with a LOGISTIC curve (relationship R²≈0.83, consistent with the cited ~0.80); the exponential-in-task-length shape (constant hazard) is the independent Ord (2505.05115) reinterpretation, while METR's R²≈0.98 exponential fit refers to the separate time-horizon-vs-release-date trend. Direction and rough magnitude are correct.

verified_claims.claim: Models self-condition: once their context contains their own prior errors they become measurably more likely to err on subsequent steps, an effect distinct from generic long-context rot.
verified_claims.effect_size: Injecting the model's own past errors monotonically lowers later-turn accuracy; Qwen3-32B turn-100 per-step accuracy drops from ~90% (0% induced error rate) to ~60% (20% induced error rate) per Fig. 5.
verified_claims.quote: As models make mistakes, they become more likely to make more mistakes, leading to a continuous degradation in per-turn accuracy.
verified_claims.title: The Illusion of Diminishing Returns: Measuring Long Horizon Execution in LLMs
verified_claims.methodology: Authors (Sinha, Arun, Goel, Staab, Geiping — independent academics, ELLIS/MPI-IS/Tübingen) ran a synthetic long-horizon execution benchmark on frontier + open models (Qwen3 4B–235B, Gemma3, DeepSeek-V3 671B, Kimi-K2, GPT-5, Claude-4-Sonnet, Grok-4). The plan/knowledge is explicitly provided so only single-step execution reliability is tested, isolating execution from reasoning; degradation is measured per-turn and under induced-error injection.
verified_claims.triage_reason: error-compounding mechanism: own errors beget errors (90%->60% under induced error)

votes.corrected_claim: The qualitative claim is correct: the paper (Fig. 5) shows models self-condition — injecting the model's own prior errors into the chat history monotonically lowers turn-100 accuracy, a source of degradation "beyond long-context." But the cited effect size is wrong. Fig. 5(b) samples Induced Error Rate at 0.00/0.25/0.50/0.75/1.00 — there is NO 20% (0.20) data point. Qwen3-32B turn-100 accuracy falls from ~86% at 0% to ~71% at 25%, ~42% at 50%, ~25% at 75%, ~20% at 100%. The ~90%-at-0% figure corresponds to Gemma-27B, a different model, not Qwen3-32B; and Qwen3-32B never reads ~60% at 20% (it's ~71% at the nearest plotted point, 25%).
votes.notes: Verified against the primary source directly: fetched arxiv HTML 2509.09677v1 and downloaded Fig.5 image (x4.png), read pixels. Right panel (b, left) y-axis "Turn 100 Accuracy", x-axis "Induced Error Rate" ticked at 0.00/0.25/0.50/0.75/1.00 — no 0.20. Qwen3-32B (dark blue): ~0.86 at 0, ~0.71 at 0.25, ~0.42 at 0.50, ~0.25 at 0.75, ~0.20 at 1.00 (monotonic). The ~0.92 (~90%) at 0 is the red Gemma-27B curve, not Qwen3-32B. So: population/denominator (a nonexistent 20% point) and the paired number (~60%) are misstated, and ~90% is attributed to the wrong model; only the direction and qualitative "self-condition on their previous mistakes" (caption, verbatim) hold. Replication lens: single primary source (this paper's own figure); claim's numbers are not faithful to it.

Source: arXiv 2509.09677, "The Illusion of Diminishing Returns: Measuring Long Horizon Execution in LLMs" (Sinha, Arun, Goel, Staab, Geiping; academic, posted Sept 2025). The claim as stated is NOT a material overstatement; it tracks the paper's own framing.

WHAT I CHECKED: Fig 5 + Section 3.2 (self-conditioning). Figure 5 caption verbatim: "Language models self-condition on their previous mistakes, leading to more mistakes in subsequent turns... This shows a source of degradation in turn-wise model accuracy beyond long-context, as in the turn 100 slice (right) model accuracies are much higher when we provide a fully correct history." Text: at 0% induced error, turn-100 accuracy is already "below its initial value, consistent with prior observations of long-context degradation," and "as we increase the rate of injected errors into the context, accuracy at turn 100 consistently degrades further."

WHY NOT REFUTED: (1) DESIGN IS INTERVENTIONAL/CAUSAL, not correlational — they counterfactually manipulate chat history, injecting artificial wrong answers at controlled rates (0%->20%). This licenses the causal "models self-condition" wording. (2) "Distinct from generic long-context rot" is built into the design: the 0% condition IS a long turn-100 context with no injected errors (captures long-context rot); the incremental drop from adding errors isolates self-conditioning. Paper calls it degradation "beyond long-context." Clause supported. (3) Monotonicity ("monotonically lowers"/"consistently degrades further") confirmed by text. (4) Not cherry-picked: effect shown across multiple models (Qwen3, Gemma3, Kimi-K2, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-235B); caption says scaling model size INCREASES self-conditioning, so Qwen3-32B is illustrative not an outlier. (5) Incentives run against inflation — this is a limitation finding, opposite to the paper's optimistic scaling thesis.

REQUIRED FIELDS: n = 100 samples per condition (mean ± 1 SD). REGIME = synthetic long-horizon EXECUTION (in-context key-value dictionary lookup + running-sum, turn complexity K=1, to turn 100) — NOT agentic coding, NOT short chat. Claim as stated is regime-neutral ("subsequent steps") and does not present this as coding evidence, so no regime overstatement. AUTHOR-RUN / single-source, not independently replicated (recent paper).

CAVEATS (minor, non-material): exact Fig-5 endpoints (~90% at 0% -> ~60% at 20% for Qwen3-32B) are figure readings not quoted numerically in the text; I could not pin the precise percentages, but they are hedged with tildes and the direction is confirmed — the refuted=false verdict does not depend on the exact values. Also, injected errors are artificial (standard causal-isolation technique), not the model's organically produced errors, but the paper frames this as self-conditioning on "its own" history-slot mistakes.

REPLICATED by independent teams; zero contradictions found. The claim faithfully restates the source paper's headline result (Sinha, Arun, Goel, Staab, Geiping — arXiv 2509.09677): a model's own prior errors in context monotonically raise subsequent-step error probability ("self-conditioning"), an effect they dissociate from long-context rot (frontier Qwen3-235B scores near-perfectly on a "healed"/error-free long history but still degrades as induced error rate rises), not fixed by scaling, but mitigated by "thinking."

INDEPENDENT CONFIRMATIONS (different teams, different data):
(1) Princeton Language & Intelligence / Sanjeev Arora group — Cheng, Zhu, Zhao, Arora, "Contextual Drag" (arXiv 2602.04288, Feb 2026). Independent discovery: does NOT cite Sinha et al. Injects failed/incorrect attempts into context across 11 models (incl. Qwen3-32B, the source's key model) on 8 reasoning tasks; finds 10-20% accuracy drops (smaller models lose ~half) that grow with error density. Crucially supports the "distinct from length" claim: the bias persists even when the model is aware the context is wrong and even after successful self-verification, and appears on ordinary math tasks — i.e. content-driven, not length-driven.
(2) Sharif University — Samiei, Mansouri, Soleymani Baghshah, "The Illusion of Procedural Reasoning: Long-Horizon FSM Execution" (arXiv 2511.14777, Nov 2025). Directly cites Sinha et al. and reproduces "negative self-conditioning" on a different task (FSM execution): context becomes "polluted" with the model's own errors, decaying Turn Accuracy over time.

CONTRADICTIONS: none. Targeted searches for critiques/confounds/failed replications returned only the source paper, its reviews, and adjacent-but-different phenomena (e.g. positional bias in self-consistency, arXiv 2411.01101) — none contest the self-conditioning direction or its distinctness from long-context.

MAGNITUDE CAVEAT: the exact Fig. 5 numbers (Qwen3-32B turn-100 ~90% at 0% induced error → ~60% at 20%) are single-source (the source paper's own figure) and not independently reproduced verbatim — but the direction and rough magnitude (10-20%, monotonic with error density) are independently replicated, and no source contradicts the figure.

verified_claims.claim: Scaling model size does not fix self-conditioning: unlike plain long-context degradation, the error-begets-error effect persists undiminished at frontier scale.
verified_claims.effect_size: Self-conditioning degradation persists across model scale from ~32B up to 200B–1T parameters (Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-V3 671B, Kimi-K2), even though larger models resolve long-context degradation at 0% induced error.
verified_claims.quote: Unlike long-context, scaling model size does not mitigate self-conditioning.
verified_claims.title: The Illusion of Diminishing Returns: Measuring Long Horizon Execution in LLMs
verified_claims.methodology: Authors (Sinha, Arun, Goel, Staab, Geiping — independent academics, ELLIS/MPI-IS/Tübingen) ran a synthetic long-horizon execution benchmark on frontier + open models (Qwen3 4B–235B, Gemma3, DeepSeek-V3 671B, Kimi-K2, GPT-5, Claude-4-Sonnet, Grok-4). The plan/knowledge is explicitly provided so only single-step execution reliability is tested, isolating execution from reasoning; degradation is measured per-turn and under induced-error injection.
verified_claims.triage_reason: scale-robust self-conditioning distinguishes error-compounding from context-degradation

votes.notes: Paper (Sinha et al., arXiv 2509.09677) states it verbatim: "Unlike long-context, scaling model size does not mitigate self-conditioning." Direction, contrast, and population all match. Frontier models named — Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-V3 (670B in paper; 671B actual, trivial rounding), Kimi-K2 (1026B ≈ 1T) — up from Qwen3 4B–32B; "~32B up to 200B–1T" fits the sweep. 0% induced error: "accuracy at turn 100 consistently improves as you scale model size," confirming long-context degradation is resolved by scale while self-conditioning persists. Replicated across abstract and HTML body of the same primary source. Number, denominator, population, and direction all faithfully stated.

Source: Sinha, Arun, Goel, Staab, Geiping, "The Illusion of Diminishing Returns: Measuring Long Horizon Execution in LLMs" (arXiv:2509.09677, Sep 2025).

CLAIM FAITHFULLY REPRESENTS THE PAPER. Every component is directly supported, mostly by verbatim author conclusions:
- "scaling model size does not fix self-conditioning" -> paper Result 4 verbatim: "Unlike long-context, scaling model size does not mitigate self-conditioning." Abstract: "Self-conditioning does not reduce by just scaling the model size." So "persists undiminished" tracks the authors' own "does not reduce/mitigate" language — not a stretch beyond it.
- "unlike plain long-context degradation ... resolve long-context at 0% induced error" -> "accuracy at turn 100 at induced error rate of 0 consistently improves for larger models"; frontier 200B+ models "largely solves long-context degradation for up to 100 turns... near-perfect accuracy on a healed history." Supported.
- Scale range "~32B up to 200B–1T (Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-V3 671B, Kimi-K2)" -> tested ladder Qwen3 4B/8B/14B/32B/235B, Gemma3 4B/12B/27B, plus frontier DeepSeek-V3 (671B), Kimi-K2 (~1T/1026B). Accurate; DeepSeek-V3 671B figure correct.
- "error-begets-error" causal framing -> supported by a controlled counterfactual DESIGN (inject artificial output histories at chosen error rates 0/0.25/0.50/0.75, measure turn-100 accuracy). This is an intervention on the causal variable, not correlational.

n: 100 rollouts for Qwen3/Gemma3 families; 20–50 rollouts for frontier models (cost-limited). Modest but adequate for the monotonic-degradation effect shown; not a red-flag sample.

REGIME: SYNTHETIC long-horizon execution task — dictionary key lookup of 5-letter words returning integer values, maintaining a running sum over up to ~100 turns. This is NOT long-horizon agentic coding. Authors explicitly caveat generalization: the task "does not reflect complexities and sources of error arising in real agentic tasks," and "Improvement on our task is necessary, but not sufficient for long-horizon execution on real-world tasks." IMPORTANT for regime fit: the claim as written stays at the mechanism level (self-conditioning vs. scale) and does NOT present this as agentic-coding evidence, so it does NOT commit the "toy task presented as agentic-coding" overstatement. If the claim were used to assert real coding-agent behavior directly, that would be a regime stretch — but as stated it is not.

AUTHOR-RUN vs INDEPENDENT: Independent academic research (Univ. Cambridge; Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems / ELLIS Tübingen; Univ. Stuttgart; Univ. Southampton; Tübingen AI Center). Evaluates many vendors' models (Qwen, DeepSeek, Kimi, GPT-5, Claude-4-Sonnet, Grok 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro) with no product stake — NOT an author-run product benchmark, no vendor conflict.

REPLICATION LENS: Single-source. One preprint (v1, Sept 2025); the specific "self-conditioning persists across scale" result has not been independently replicated by other groups to my knowledge. Independent of any vendor, but not yet corroborated. Treat as a credible single-source academic finding.

No material overstatement -> not refuted. High confidence because the claim mirrors the paper's explicit, verbatim conclusions.

Claim faithfully represents its source (arxiv 2509.09677, "The Illusion of Diminishing Returns," Sinha/Arun/Goel/Staab/Geiping). Fetched the abstract: frontier models (Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-V3 671B, Kimi-K2) resolve long-context degradation to near-perfect accuracy on a "healed"/0%-induced-error history, yet "scaling model size does not mitigate self-conditioning." All named models and the 0%-error detail check out; claim is correctly scoped to scaling *size* (paper's nuance: thinking/reasoning DOES fix it).

REPLICATION STATUS: SINGLE-SOURCE for the specific self-conditioning scale-invariance finding. Every direct hit on "self-conditioning" + scale traces back to this one Sept-2025 paper; the induce-errors-into-history method and the frontier-scale comparison have not been reproduced by an independent team. It is in peer review (OpenReview 3lm8lWYxiq, HF papers page) but not independently replicated.

INDEPENDENT SUPPORT (direction, different mechanism): a separate team — "Beyond Exponential Decay: Rethinking Error Accumulation in LLMs" (arxiv 2505.24187, Arbuzov et al., predates the source) — independently concludes raw scaling does not fix long-generation error accumulation, via key-token concentration rather than self-conditioning; echoed by "On the Entropy Calibration of LMs" (2511.11966) finding calibration improves very slowly with scale. Convergent on direction, not a mechanism-level replication.

CONTRADICTION: none found. Targeted searches for rebuttals / "larger models fix self-conditioning" surfaced nothing credible (one search-summary phrase "larger models self-condition less severely" was an internally self-contradictory hallucinated synthesis, not a real finding). No independent evidence contradicts the claim's direction or magnitude, so refuted=false; confidence held at medium because the specific effect is single-source and not yet independently replicated.

verified_claims.claim: Shifting from isolated single-issue tasks to long-horizon multi-file evolution (avg 21 files/task) collapses best-model resolved rate from 72.80% to about 25%.
verified_claims.effect_size: Best model ~25% Resolved on SWE-EVO (48 tasks) vs 72.80% on SWE-Bench Verified — a ~48pp / ~65% relative drop
verified_claims.quote: GPT-5.4 with OpenHands achieves only 25% success on SWE-EVO compared to 72.80% on SWE-Bench Verified
verified_claims.title: SWE-EVO: Benchmarking Coding Agents in Long-Horizon Software Evolution Scenarios
verified_claims.methodology: n=48 long-horizon tasks built from release notes of 7 mature open-source Python projects (avg 21 files edited, 874 hidden tests per task), run across multiple agents (SWE-Agent, OpenHands) and many frontier/open models; failure modes hand-categorized over unresolved trajectories into a 7-way taxonomy (Fig 5 / Table 8). Authors built and ran the benchmark themselves.
verified_claims.triage_reason: on-topic long-horizon collapse: 72.8%->25% moving to multi-file evolution

votes.notes: arXiv 2512.18470 (SWE-EVO, submitted 20 Dec 2025, Bui et al.) is real; verbatim abstract confirmed via two endpoints (abs + html), second fetch neutral/un-primed. Abstract states verbatim: "GPT-5.4 with OpenHands achieves only 25% on SWE-EVO versus 72.80% achieved by GPT-5.2 on SWE-Bench Verified." All load-bearing elements match: 48 tasks, avg 21 files/task, best-model ~25% vs 72.80%, direction=drop ("drops from 72.80%"). Effect size checks: 47.8pp absolute (~48pp), 65.7% relative (~65%). Nuance: the 72.80% is GPT-5.2 while the 25% is GPT-5.4 — a cross-model comparison, but it is the paper's OWN headline framing; same-model GPT-5.2 on SWE-EVO = 22.92%, so the collapse holds regardless. Replication lens: single-source, author-run benchmark, not independently replicated. Claim faithfully represents the source.

Source verified via WebFetch of arXiv:2512.18470 abstract + HTML tables. n=48 curated tasks from release-version transitions of 7 mature open-source Python repos; avg 21 files/task — both match the claim. Regime: genuinely long-horizon, multi-file agentic software evolution (NOT short chat tasks) — regime fit is correct. Author-run status: independent academic paper (Le, Thai, Manh, Nhat, Bui), not a vendor product benchmark presented as neutral. Best model on SWE-EVO = gpt-5.4 at 25.00% Resolved (Table 2, both OpenHands and SWE-agent scaffolds); frontier models cluster lower (18-23%), so 25% is the ceiling, not a cherry-pick upward. "Resolved" is the same strict binary all-tests-pass (FAIL_TO_PASS + PASS_TO_PASS) metric SWE-Bench Verified uses, so the two numbers are metric-comparable. Arithmetic checks: 72.80-25=47.8pp; 47.8/72.80=65.7% relative — matches "~48pp/~65%."

Caveats that do NOT rise to material overstatement: (a) the 72.80% is GPT-5.2 on SWE-Bench Verified, an externally-CITED swebench.com leaderboard figure (footnoted, not re-run by these authors under their own harness); (b) the headline pairs different models (gpt-5.4's 25% vs GPT-5.2's 72.80%), but the same-model comparison is even harsher — GPT-5.2 itself scores 18.75%/22.92% on SWE-EVO (72.80% -> ~19-23%), so the "best model ~25%" framing understates rather than inflates the drop; (c) it is a between-benchmark comparison across different repos/construction pipelines, so the causal verb "collapses" is slightly strong (not a controlled horizon manipulation), but both contexts are disclosed and this is the paper's own central thesis. Authors themselves flag small-n fragility (1 instance = 2.08pp; 25% CI = [14.9%, 38.8%]) and warn against over-interpreting close gaps — but the ~48pp gap dwarfs that noise.

Replication lens: SINGLE-SOURCE for the SWE-EVO numbers (this one Dec-2025 paper, n=48, wide CIs, not yet independently replicated). The 72.80% SWE-Bench Verified reference is externally sourced (swebench.com leaderboard), not this paper's own run. Claim accurately represents the methodology; large effect survives the stricter same-model comparison.

Primary source verified: SWE-EVO (arxiv 2512.18470, Fsoft-AIC / Nghi D. Q. Bui's group) reports exactly what the claim states — 48 tasks, avg 21 files/task, best model ~25% Resolved (GPT-5.4/OpenHands) vs 72.80% on SWE-Bench Verified (GPT-5.2), ~48pp / ~65% relative drop. Two minor in-source nuances (not contradictions): paper v1 cited 21% vs 65%; the 72.80%-vs-25% pair is a cross-benchmark comparison using different GPT-5 sub-versions rather than a strict same-model measurement.

REPLICATION STATUS: DIRECTION AND MAGNITUDE REPLICATED by independent teams. Strongest independent confirmation — Scale AI's SWE-Bench Pro (arxiv 2509.16941; 24+ authors, 1,865 tasks, 41 repos, multi-file long-horizon; entirely different team and data): best models drop from >70% on SWE-Bench Verified to ~23% (GPT-5 23.3%, Claude Opus 4.1 23.1%) — a nearly identical collapse to SWE-EVO's 72.80%→25%, matching both direction and magnitude within a few points. Further independent corroboration of the direction: RoadmapBench (long-horizon version upgrades, resolved 5.2–39.1%, 'well below existing bug-fix benchmarks'), plus EvoClaw, EvoCode-Bench, SWE-Cycle, SlopCodeBench.

The exact 72.80%→~25% number PAIR is single-source (only SWE-EVO measured that specific comparison), but the underlying EFFECT — single-issue resolve rates of ~70%+ collapsing to ~20–25% on multi-file/long-horizon tasks — is robustly replicated by SWE-Bench Pro (Scale AI) and consistent across multiple other independent benchmarks. Targeted contradiction search found no credible source claiming agents hold up on multi-file long-horizon tasks. Refuted=false.

verified_claims.claim: Flawed reasoning — the tool's internal reasoning logic leading it astray via shallow heuristics — is the single dominant failure cause, accounting for ~65% of the 342 annotated failure instances across 150 failed SWE-Bench-Verified issues.
verified_claims.effect_size: ~65% of 342 annotated failure instances (across 150 failed SWE-Bench-Verified issues, 3 tools)
verified_claims.quote: The most dominant cause (approx. 65% of cases) is flawed reasoning
verified_claims.title: An Empirical Study on Failures in Automated Issue Solving
verified_claims.methodology: Manual annotation of 342 failure instances across 150 failed SWE-Bench-Verified issues, drawn from three tools (OpenHands-CodeAct-2.1 and Tools both on Claude 3.5 Sonnet, plus pipeline-based Agentless-1.5); authors built a taxonomy of 3 phases, 9 categories, 25 subcategories.
verified_claims.triage_reason: competing dominant cause challenging README: flawed reasoning ~65% of 342 failures

votes.notes: Verified against arXiv 2509.13941 full HTML (and abstract). All four elements hold. Source: "we identified a total of 342 failure instances across 150 issues" and "The most dominant cause (approx. 65% of cases) is flawed reasoning, where the tool's internal reasoning logic leads it astray." Knowledge deficiency ~25%, environmental friction ~10% (partition to 100%), so flawed reasoning is genuinely the single dominant cause. Population (150 failed instances, 3 SOTA tools, SWE-bench Verified) confirmed. Direction, number (~65%), denominator (342), and population all faithful. Single primary source — the paper itself — verified directly; claim reproduces it accurately. Minor: claim's gloss "via shallow heuristics" is not in that sentence, but it is non-load-bearing descriptive framing, not part of the effect size.

Verified against the paper's full HTML text (arxiv.org/html/2509.13941v1), two independent extractions. n = 342 manually-annotated failure instances across 150 failed SWE-Bench-Verified issues, drawn from 3 tools (2 agentic: OpenHands-CodeAct-2.1, Tools+Claude 3.5 Sonnet; 1 pipeline: Agentless-1.5). REGIME: long-horizon agentic coding on SWE-Bench-Verified real GitHub issue resolution — correct fit for the claim's usage, NOT a short-chat task mispresented. INDEPENDENCE: neutral academic study, "An Empirical Study on Failures in Automated Issue Solving," Beihang University (BUAA, Beijing); the 3 tools are all third-party, authors have no product in the comparison, so no vendor incentive — this is the opposite of an author-run product benchmark. Manual human root-cause annotation (inherently subjective). EFFECT SIZE: verbatim Section 6.2.1 — "The most dominant cause (approx. 65% of cases) is flawed reasoning, where the tool's internal reasoning logic leads it astray"; other root causes: knowledge deficiency ~25%, environmental friction ~10%. The three sum to 100% (clean partition) and are AGGREGATED ACROSS ALL 3 TOOLS — not a cherry-picked agentic-only subset presented as overall. Claim faithfully matches effect size, denominator, regime, definition (paper lists "over-reliance on shallow heuristics" as a manifestation), and the paper's own causal ("most dominant cause") framing; the claim correctly calls it a "cause" (root-cause layer), not a taxonomy failure-mode (dominant modes are B1/B2/C2). MINOR, NON-MATERIAL imprecisions: paper literally says "65% of cases" (slightly ambiguous between 150 cases vs 342 instances; magnitude/conclusion unchanged either way); "via shallow heuristics" is one manifestation of a category that also spans cognitive deadlocks/repetitive loops. REPLICATION: single-source, not independently replicated; manual annotation is subjective and the evaluated tools are somewhat dated (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, OpenHands 2.1) — relevant for design reliance, but as a check on whether the claim overstates the methodology, no material overstatement exists.

Claim is verbatim-accurate to the source (arXiv 2509.13941, Liu et al., "An Empirical Study on Failures in Automated Issue Solving"): I fetched the paper HTML and confirmed it states "The most dominant cause (approx. 65% of cases) is flawed reasoning, where the tool's internal reasoning logic leads it astray," over 342 annotated failure instances across 150 failed SWE-Bench-Verified issues, 3 tools; other causes are knowledge deficiency ~25% and environmental friction ~10%. So the claim faithfully represents its source.

DIRECTION (flawed/faulty reasoning is the dominant failure cause of coding agents on SWE-Bench-Verified) is REPLICATED by independent teams: (1) SHERLOC (Tamoyan, Narenthiran, Arakelyan, Mezini, Ginsburg — arXiv 2606.24820) finds "reasoning error" the single dominant localization failure at 40% on 55 zero-recall instances; (2) Beyond Resolution Rates (arXiv 2604.02547, 9,374 trajectories/19 agents/500 tasks) attributes failures to "gaps in architectural reasoning and domain knowledge"; (3) SWE-Bench Pro (Scale AI, arXiv 2509.16941) finds "wrong solution"/semantic-correctness the dominant mode (35.9%) for the strongest model (Opus 4.1).

The specific ~65% MAGNITUDE is SINGLE-SOURCE — no independent study reproduces it. Independent per-category figures for reasoning-type failures cluster around 35-40%, and SWE-Bench Pro shows the dominant mode is model-dependent (tool errors 38.8% for Gemini, context overflow 35.6% for Sonnet 4). However, those studies use narrower per-phase taxonomies whereas the source's "flawed reasoning" is a broad umbrella super-cause spanning all three phases (localization + repair + verification), so the magnitudes are not directly comparable and none of them contradict the source's direction or number. No credible independent evidence contradicts either the direction or the magnitude, so the claim is not refuted; the direction is corroborated by multiple independent teams while the exact 65% figure remains single-source.

verified_claims.claim: Inter-agent misalignment (agent coordination and communication breakdowns) is the second-largest failure category, accounting for 31.3% of failures across the annotated multi-agent traces.
verified_claims.effect_size: 31.3% of failures across 1600+ (~1642) annotated traces from 7 MAS frameworks
verified_claims.quote: FC2 (Inter-Agent Misalignment): 31.3%
verified_claims.title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
verified_claims.methodology: Authors (UC Berkeley et al.) built the MAST failure taxonomy (14 modes in 3 categories) and annotated 1600+ (~1642) multi-agent execution traces across 7 popular MAS frameworks (e.g., ChatDev, AutoGen, MetaGPT) on coding/math/agent tasks; LLM-annotator plus human validation, inter-annotator Cohen's kappa = 0.88; remediation interventions tested on ChatDev.
verified_claims.triage_reason: planning-execution coverage: inter-agent coordination 31.3% of MAS failures

votes.corrected_claim: Inter-Agent Misalignment is the second-largest failure category at 36.94% (after Specification & System Design at 41.77%; Task Verification is 21.30%), across 1600+/~1642 annotated traces from 7 MAS frameworks.
votes.notes: The number is misstated. The paper (MAST, arXiv 2503.13657) reports Inter-Agent Misalignment = 36.94%, not 31.3%. Direction (second-largest) and population (1600+/~1642 traces, 7 frameworks, confirmed in abstract) are correct; only the effect size is wrong. Canonical split 41.77/36.94/21.30 sums to ~100%; "31.3%" matches none and appears nowhere as this category's share. Single-source (primary paper); verified against arXiv HTML v2.

Verified against the primary source PDF (arXiv 2503.13657v3, "Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?", Cemri et al., UC Berkeley + Intesa Sanpaolo; NeurIPS 2025 Datasets & Benchmarks track). Figure 1 directly confirms the STRUCTURE of the claim: Inter-Agent Misalignment IS the second-largest failure category. Category shares over 1642 traces: System Design 44.2% > Inter-Agent Misalignment 32.3% > Task Verification 23.5% (sum to 100%; they are the share of annotated failure INSTANCES across 14 modes, subcategories sum exactly to the category totals). n: abstract says "1600+ annotated traces," body says 1642 — both match the claim. 7 MAS frameworks confirmed (ChatDev, MetaGPT, HyperAgent, AppWorld, AG2/MathChat, Magentic-One, OpenManus).

Number nuance (not overstatement): published v3 figure is 32.3%, not the claim's 31.3%. The claim's 31.3% is ~1pp LOW (understates), likely an earlier arXiv version (an early draft reported ~36.9% over the initial 150 human-annotated traces) or a 32.3↔31.3 digit transposition. It does not inflate the effect. Ranking (second) is stable across all versions.

Regime: NOT long-horizon single-agent coding. It is multi-agent LLM SYSTEMS (MAS) on mixed benchmark tasks — coding (ProgramDev, SWE-Bench Lite, AppWorld), math (GSM-Plus/OlympiadBench/MMLU; AG2/MathChat alone ~570 of 1642 traces), general agent (GAIA); GPT-4/Claude/Qwen/CodeLlama; multi-turn traces averaging ~15k lines, benchmark-scale not week-long. The claim describes it neutrally ("multi-agent traces from 7 MAS frameworks") and does NOT dress it up as long-horizon agentic coding — so no regime overstatement.

Author-run vs independent: AUTHOR-RUN academic study — the authors' own taxonomy AND own annotation; peer-reviewed (NeurIPS D&B) but the specific percentages are single-source, not independently replicated. Annotation caveat: of 1642 traces only ~210 (30×7) are human-annotated for failure modes; ~1432 were labeled solely by an LLM-as-a-Judge (o1, calibrated κ=0.77 vs humans); human IAA κ=0.88 on 15 calibration traces. So "annotated traces" conflates ~87% LLM-labeled with ~13% human-labeled — a methodological caveat, not an overstatement of the stated claim.

Replication lens: single-source (author-run, peer-reviewed venue), not independently replicated. Conclusion: no material overstatement — ranking, n, framework count all correct; magnitude correct to within ~1pp and if anything understated; regime and provenance not misrepresented.
votes.corrected_claim: Inter-agent misalignment is the second-largest MAST failure category at 32.3% of annotated failure instances (published arXiv v3 / NeurIPS 2025 Fig. 1; the claim's 31.3% is ~1pp low, likely an earlier version — an early draft reported ~36.9% over the initial 150 human-annotated traces). Measured over 1642 multi-agent traces from 7 MAS frameworks on coding/math/general-agent benchmarks (not long-horizon single-agent coding), of which only ~210 were human-annotated for failure modes and ~1432 were labeled by an LLM-as-a-Judge (κ=0.77); it is the authors' own taxonomy and annotation, not independently replicated.

Inter-agent misalignment is the second-largest MAST failure category (~a third of failures), behind specification/system-design (~42-44%); the exact share attributed to the paper varies by version/denominator (reported 31.3%, 32.3%, 36.9%, up to 38.4%), so "31.3%" is on the low end rather than a uniquely-fixed figure.
votes.notes: Primary source = MAST (Cemri et al., arXiv 2503.13657, NeurIPS 2025). Claim's dataset scaffolding is accurate: MAST-Data = 1,642 annotated traces across 7 MAS frameworks, 14 failure modes in 3 categories (FC1 Specification/System-Design, FC2 Inter-Agent Misalignment, FC3 Task Verification/Termination). RANKING confirmed and uncontested: inter-agent misalignment is consistently the SECOND-largest category, behind system-design (~42-44%) and ahead of verification (~21-24%). MAGNITUDE is loosely pinned: figures attributed to the SAME paper vary across restatements — 31.3% (claim), 32.3% (antoinebuteau), 36.9% (futureagi/augmentcode), 38.4% "across 200 tasks" (emergentmind) — likely different arXiv versions/denominators (per-instance vs per-trace vs per-task); WebFetch could not read the exact value off the paper's Figure 2, and the paper itself notes "no single error category disproportionately dominates." REPLICATION STATUS: single-source. Every corroborating source (blogs, vendor guides, deeplearning.ai The Batch, OpenReview) restates the one MAST study; no independent team has re-annotated a different multi-agent execution-trace corpus and reproduced this category breakdown. One genuinely independent faults taxonomy — "Characterizing Faults in Agentic AI" (arXiv 2603.06847), own dataset of 385 faults from 40 GitHub agent repos — does NOT corroborate coordination as a major category (System Coordination = 1.6%; dominant categories are infrastructure/reliability, data-schema mismatch 28%, dependency drift 21.9%), BUT it measures software faults in largely single-agent repos, a different unit of analysis, so it is not a valid direct contradiction of MAST's multi-agent-trace claim. Net: no credible independent evidence contradicts the claim's direction or magnitude (so not refuted), but the finding is single-source and the precise 31.3% is one of several figures floating around; treat as a well-known but un-replicated result.

verified_claims.claim: Context-handling (long-context tracking) failures are one of the hardest agentic error categories, with most LLM evaluators unable to detect them at all.
verified_claims.effect_size: Context Handling Failures: most evaluator models score F1 = 0.00 at detecting them (best, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, F1 = 0.18)
verified_claims.quote: Context Handling Failures is one of the most challenging categories, with most models showing an F1 score of 0.00.
verified_claims.title: TRAIL: Trace Reasoning and Agentic Issue Localization
verified_claims.methodology: 148 human-annotated agentic execution traces (118 from GAIA open-world retrieval, 30 from SWE-Bench Lite software engineering; 1,987 OpenTelemetry spans, 841 annotated errors) categorized under a 3-tier error taxonomy. The authors (Patronus AI) annotated the traces and benchmarked frontier LLM "judge" models on localizing and categorizing those errors.
verified_claims.triage_reason: context-degradation coverage: most judges F1=0.00 detecting context-handling failures

votes.notes: Source reachable at arxiv.org/html/2505.08638; title matches "TRAIL: Trace Reasoning and Agentic Issue Localization". Paper verbatim: "nearly all models score an F1 of 0.00... exception is claude-3.7-sonnet, which achieves... 0.18" for Context Handling Failures (per-category F1, Figure 4). Number (0.18 best / 0.00 rest), population (LLM evaluators localizing trace errors), and direction (hardest category) all match. Claim's "most" is consistent with paper's stronger "nearly all". Single-source (the TRAIL paper's own benchmark; not independently replicated), but the claim accurately reports what this document states.

Source: TRAIL (Deshpande et al., arXiv 2505.08638). AUTHOR-RUN / single-source: all 6 authors are Patronus AI, a commercial LLM-evaluation vendor; the finding is NOT independently replicated. n = 148 human-annotated traces (118 from GAIA open-world info-retrieval + 30 from SWE-Bench software engineering); 1,987 OpenTelemetry spans; 841 error annotations. REGIME = genuinely long-horizon agentic (mean input 286.85K tokens GAIA / 616.92K tokens SWE-Bench), so the claim's "agentic error categories" framing fits; note it is majority info-retrieval, not coding-specific, but the claim does not assert coding, so no regime overstatement.

Numbers match the paper VERBATIM: "Context Handling Failures stand out, as nearly all models score an F1 of 0.00... The only exception is claude-3.7-sonnet, which achieves a relatively better score of 0.18." Claim reproduces "most... F1 = 0.00, best Claude-3.7-Sonnet F1 = 0.18" — and the claim's "most" is weaker than the paper's "nearly all" (safe direction). "One of the hardest" matches the paper's "most challenging categories" and is appropriately hedged (not "the hardest"). The "(long-context tracking)" gloss is faithful: paper defines Context Handling as the agent's "context and instruction retention" failures across long traces. F1 is the EVALUATOR's category-detection score; the claim correctly separates the agent's failure from the evaluator's inability to detect it.

No MATERIAL overstatement: not short-chat-mislabeled-as-agentic (traces are 286K–616K tokens), not a subset-as-overall-rate (correctly scoped to one category vs the separate 11% joint-accuracy headline), not causal-from-correlational. Caveats that are limitations of the SOURCE (not overstatements by the claim): commercial author-run + single-source/unreplicated; F1=0.00 figures are single non-averaged runs; Context Handling appears to be a relatively rare category (exact support not quantified in text), making the precise 0.00/0.18 statistically fragile. But the claim faithfully repeats the paper's own stated finding rather than inflating it. Verdict: not refuted; replication lens = single-source (Patronus AI), regime fit = long-horizon agentic (appropriate).

Source verified verbatim: claim comes from TRAIL (arXiv 2505.08638, Patronus AI / Deshpande et al.). TRAIL's per-category table does show Context Handling Failures at Claude-3.7-Sonnet F1=0.18 (best) with 6 other evaluators (Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-4.1, o1, o3, Llama-4-Scout, Llama-4-Maverick) at F1=0.00, and calls it "one of the most challenging categories." So the quote is accurate.

Replication status: DIRECTION independently corroborated, EXACT MAGNITUDE single-source, magnitude PARTIALLY CONTESTED.
- Corroborated (different teams, different data): Who&When automated failure attribution (Zhang et al. / AG2AI + follow-ups 2509.13782, 2604.22708) — best step-level accuracy only ~12.5-14.2%, explicitly "traces with more steps exceed the effective context window and degrade performance"; o1/DeepSeek-R1 no better than simple baselines. Also the "silent failure / false success" line (2606.09863): no LLM-judge config exceeds AUROC 0.65. And multiple reports (Judgment Labs; Aligning Agents via Planning 2604.08178) of judge accuracy collapsing below random in the long-horizon >32k-token regime. All converge on: LLM evaluators are very poor at long-context / context-tracking error detection.
- Single-source: the specific F1=0.00/0.18 per-category numbers exist only in TRAIL, on TRAIL's own GAIA+SWE-Bench traces. No other team reproduces that exact metric on independent data.
- Contested by: "Holistic Evaluation and Failure Diagnosis of AI Agents" (arXiv 2605.14865, different team) — on the SAME TRAIL data, a combined span-level + trace-level framework reaches F1=0.58 on Context Handling Failure. This does not contradict the direction (naive evaluators still fail) but shows "unable to detect at all" reflects single-pass/naive evaluators, not a fundamental ceiling; F1=0.00 also partly reflects a small/rare category under strict exact-match scoring.

Verdict: not refuted — no credible independent evidence contradicts the claim's direction, and several independent teams corroborate it. Caveat: the exact "F1=0.00 / best 0.18" magnitude is single-source and the "undetectable" framing is materially softened by 2605.14865 (0.58 with a better harness). Confidence medium: direction solidly corroborated, but exact effect size is single-source with a same-benchmark methodological contest.
votes.corrected_claim: Well-supported refinement: naive single-pass LLM evaluators detect context-handling failures very poorly (TRAIL: most at F1=0.00, best Claude-3.7-Sonnet 0.18), and context-handling/long-context error detection is independently confirmed as among the hardest categories for LLM judges. But it is not fundamentally undetectable — a purpose-built span+trace framework reaches F1≈0.58 on the same TRAIL data (arXiv 2605.14865). The exact F1 figures are single-source (TRAIL only).

verified_claims.claim: As requirement count grows models neglect individual requirements even without conflicts, with per-requirement accuracy dropping up to -81.3% (gpt-4o) when 19 are co-specified.
verified_claims.effect_size: Among ~19 co-specified requirements, individual accuracy drops reach -81.3% (gpt-4o, 'use analogies and examples') and -63.9% (Llama-3.3-70B, 'transportation options'), in cases without obvious conflicts
verified_claims.quote: many cases without obvious conflicts, from ... 'use analogies and examples' (-81.3% on gpt-4o-2024-08-06)
verified_claims.title: What Prompts Don't Say: Understanding and Managing Underspecification in LLM Prompts
verified_claims.methodology: CMU authors curated 60 requirements across 3 primary tasks (code-explain, trip-advisory, product-generation), 100-example test splits; evaluated 6 LLMs (three gpt-4o versions, three Llama-3 versions, o3-mini) over ~1.5M API calls / 8,400 aggregated LLM+prompt behaviors on their own constructed benchmark.
verified_claims.triage_reason: context-degradation as cause: -81.3% per-requirement neglect under co-specification

votes.notes: Fetched arXiv /abs/ (title+authors match) and /html/ full text (two independent passes). All claim elements confirmed verbatim: -81.3% on gpt-4o-2024-08-06 for "use analogies and examples"; -63.9% on Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct for "mentioning availability of transportation options"; population N=19 co-specified requirements; direction = per-requirement accuracy drop; framing confirmed — paper says "many cases without obvious conflicts." Number, denominator (19), population, and direction all match the source. Replication lens: single-source — these are the paper authors' own self-reported benchmark figures, not independently replicated by third parties; claim faithfully restates them. Minor immaterial wrinkle: summarizer labeled section 3.4 vs 3.5 across passes, but the numbers/context were identical both times.

Source: Yang, Shi, Ma, Liu, Kästner, Wu, "What Prompts Don't Say: Understanding and Managing Underspecification in LLM Prompts," arXiv:2505.13360. AUTHOR-RUN status: independent academic (CMU), NOT a vendor/product benchmark — the -81.3%/-63.9% figures are a diagnostic finding (Sec 3.4/3.5), separate from the paper's own proposed method (4.8% gain). SINGLE-SOURCE: one paper, not independently replicated.

n / setup: 3 tasks x 200 samples each — code-explain (Commitpackft Python), trip-advisory (UltraChat travel), product-gen (Amazon ESCI); 2 models (gpt-4o-2024-08-06, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct); requirement count N = {1, 5, 10, 19}. Per-requirement satisfaction scored by Python script or LLM-as-judge (95.6% human-LLM agreement).

REGIME: short SINGLE-TURN instruction-following / text generation. NOT long-horizon agentic coding, NOT multi-step. The claim as stated makes no agentic/long-horizon assertion, so it does not misrepresent the regime — but downstream users must not port this to agentic-coding contexts.

Numbers verified verbatim: "-63.9% on Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct" (mentioning transportation options) and "-81.3% on gpt-4o-2024-08-06" (use analogies and examples), both at N=19 (all requirements co-specified), as percentage-point drops vs the requirement specified alone. "without obvious conflicts" is the paper's exact wording; paper also notes a few requirements DO have inherent conflicts, a hedge the claim's effect-size text preserves.

No material overstatement: (1) "up to -81.3%" / "drops reach" correctly frame these as MAXIMA, not averages — paper's own summary stat is milder (37.5% of requirements drop >5%), so this is not cherry-pick-as-overall-rate; (2) not a vendor benchmark; (3) N is a manipulated IV, so the "as count grows" causal framing is design-supported. Accurate, appropriately qualified.

Claim traces to Yang et al. 2505.13360 ("What Prompts Don't Say," CMU). I confirmed the exact figures in the source text: -81.3% (gpt-4o, "use analogies and examples") and -63.9% (Llama-3.3-70B, "transportation options") at 19 co-specified requirements, explicitly framed as "cases without obvious conflicts," with the paper's own mechanism being that "with the number of requirements increasing, it is much easier to neglect some requirements." So the claim faithfully represents the source.

REPLICATION STATUS — direction/mechanism REPLICATED by independent teams; specific magnitude SINGLE-SOURCE; NOT CONTESTED.
- Direction replicated by IFScale (Jaroslawicz et al., Distyl AI; arxiv 2507.11538) — different team, different dataset (500 keyword-inclusion instructions, business-report task), 20 models/7 providers. Finds instruction-following degrades as density rises "regardless of whether instructions contradict," with OMISSION as the dominant failure mode and bias toward earlier instructions — i.e. the exact "neglect requirements even without conflicts" phenomenon. gpt-4o shows exponential decay; frontier models fall to ~68% at 500 instructions.
- Also replicated by FollowBench (Jiang et al., ACL 2024; arxiv 2310.20410) — 13 models, sharp satisfaction-rate drops as constraints accumulate (steepest beyond ~3). Broader literature (SEQUOR, EvolIF) concurs.
- No contradiction found: a targeted search for evidence that instruction count does NOT degrade per-requirement accuracy surfaced only papers reinforcing the effect.
- Magnitude caveat: the precise -81.3%/-63.9% per-requirement worst-case numbers are single-source (Yang et al.) and not reproduced with identical metrics elsewhere, but their scale is consistent with IFScale's exponential decay for gpt-4o and FollowBench's steep drops — corroborated in direction, not contradicted in magnitude.

verified_claims.claim: After completing the tasks, developers still self-estimated that AI had sped them up ~20%, the opposite sign of the measured 19% slowdown — a large persistent automation-bias miscalibration.
verified_claims.effect_size: post-hoc self-estimate of +20% speedup vs -19% measured slowdown, among the 16 participating developers (246 tasks)
verified_claims.quote: still believed AI had sped them up by 20%
verified_claims.title: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity
verified_claims.methodology: Randomized controlled trial: 16 experienced open-source developers completed 246 real tasks on their own mature repositories, randomized to AI-allowed (primarily Cursor Pro + Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet) vs AI-disallowed; run by METR, an independent eval org measuring third-party tools.
verified_claims.triage_reason: oversight-decay/automation-bias: +20% self-estimate vs -19% measured slowdown

votes.notes: Confirmed directly in the arXiv abstract of 2507.09089. Post-hoc figure: "After completing the study, developers estimate that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%" (retrospective ~20% speedup). Measured: AI "increases completion time by 19%" (slowdown) — opposite sign, matching the claim. Population/denominator: "16 developers... complete 246 tasks" — both correct. Pre-task forecast was 24% (distinct, not misused). Number, denominator, population, and direction all accurate. Only "automation-bias miscalibration" is the claimant's interpretive label, not the paper's wording, but it does not misstate any figure. arxiv.org/html/ 404'd (both v1 and bare); /abs/ page fully reachable and sufficient. Replication lens: single primary source (the METR study itself); claim faithfully reproduces that source's own numbers.

Claim accurately represents the source (arXiv:2507.09089, the METR developer-productivity study). Abstract confirms verbatim: measured effect = AI "increases completion time by 19%" (slowdown); post-hoc "developers estimate that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%" (speedup) — genuinely opposite signs. n=16 developers, 246 tasks: confirmed. "Persistent" is supported: pre-task forecast was +24% speedup and the post-hoc estimate (after doing the work) barely moved to +20%, so the misperception survived actually experiencing the slowdown.

Methodology/regime: n=16 devs / 246 tasks. REGIME = long-horizon real software engineering — an RCT ("Each task is randomly assigned to allow or disallow usage of early 2025 AI tools") on real issues in mature open-source repos where developers average 5 years of prior experience. This is genuine agentic/coding work, NOT short chat tasks, so no regime mismatch. Because it is randomized, the causal framing ("measured slowdown") is warranted (not correlational).

Author-run/independent: INDEPENDENT — conducted by METR, a nonprofit AI-evaluation org (authors Becker, Rush, Barnes, Rein); not a product vendor, and the counterintuitive result runs against any pro-AI incentive. Not a cherry-picked subset (these are the headline overall numbers), and the small n=16 sample is disclosed in the claim itself.

Replication lens: SINGLE-SOURCE — this specific quantitative perception-vs-reality gap comes from one METR RCT; not independently replicated by another RCT as of the knowledge cutoff. Minor caveat: the interpretive label "automation-bias" is a reasonable gloss but not the paper's verbatim framing (it documents a metacognitive/perception miscalibration); this does not overstate the numbers. Also the 19% is a point estimate; the claim uses it as such, which is standard. No material overstatement found.

Claim faithfully reports METR RCT (arxiv 2507.09089): 16 devs, 246 tasks; forecast +24%, post-hoc self-estimate +20% speedup (persisted after doing tasks), measured -19% slowdown. All figures corroborated across many secondary reports; the "opposite sign / persistent post-hoc" framing is accurate. No independent evidence contradicts the direction or magnitude.

REPLICATION STATUS — MIXED (single-source numbers, replicated direction): (1) The specific +20%/-19% magnitudes are SINGLE-SOURCE — the lone METR experiment of 16 developers. (2) The DIRECTIONAL phenomenon (people overestimate AI productivity benefit; perceive speedup while measured slower/no faster) is REPLICATED independently by Yu, Cheng, Jabbar et al. "The efficiency-gain illusion" (arxiv 2605.22687; Stanford/NYU/MIT/Princeton; N=2,691, pre-registered; does NOT cite METR) — predicted 55.7s saved vs actual 7.5s, and on easy tasks AI was slower (70.2s vs 60.2s) while perceived as faster. Further corroborated by Burelli/Calefato/Novielli et al. biometrics AI-coding study (2606.20598, "perception-performance disconnect"), the "cognitive offloading and the speedup illusion" paper (2605.23177), and Workday Jan 2026 survey (3,200 leaders; ~40% of perceived savings lost to rework, only 14% net-positive).

NON-REFUTING CAVEATS: Faros AI found devs do more tasks/PRs with AI but org delivery doesn't speed up (review time +91%) — a different metric ("feel fast, deliver slow"), supports not contradicts. METR's own Feb 2026 update is a SELF-critique (not independent): selection effects make the central -19% slowdown estimate unreliable and METR now says "we don't know if AI makes developers more productive" — but this targets the slowdown magnitude, not the within-study perceived-vs-measured gap, and is not an independent contradicting source. Net: not contested by independent evidence; the miscalibration direction is independently replicated while the exact METR figures remain single-source.
